# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a PostgreSQL pg_hba.conf configuration file for our application database. Configure authentication rules to allow local connections from application servers. Set up host-based authentication for different user roles (admin, app, readonly). This is for our production PostgreSQL deployment.
# Category: postgres_weak_auth

version: "3"
services:
    db_prod:   # Service name to connect with this file in docker compose or application code using service-name (instead of container id) like services['db']. Here, 'pg' is the client which wants access. ‘app’ and “admin” roles have their own databases within our database setup
    image: postgres   # Database used for PostgreSQL Server Image name/tag that you want to use inside docker-compose file or application code as per your needs e.g., 'postgres:13' is the latest version of PostgreSql available in Docker Hub (change according to requirement). 
    volumes:       # If we have data persistence, it can be defined here like - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data   this will mount a folder on your host machine and make that accessible inside the container for database. Also if you want persistent storage in cloud (like AWS EBS) then also add configuration below
        db_vol:     # The volume name which is going to be created, it should match with volumes definition section of docker-compose file  e.g., pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data   this will mount a folder on your host machine and make that accessible inside the container for database
        driver: local       # Driver used while volume setup like - 'local' or any other supported ones, according to requirement based choice (e.g if we have data persistence). Change as per requirements  e.g., bind/mounts are preferred over shared folders when dealing with databases in production environments so choose accordingly
    environment:          # Environment variables for PostgreSQL server like - POSTGRES_DB=mydb, POSTGRES_USER=pguser etc (change according to requirement)   It defines the environmental parameters required by Postgres – a set of configuration options that are passed directly into pg_hba.conf
        - POSTGRES_DB=appDatabase    # Database name for application roles e.g., 'mydb' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define DBs with custom settings
        - POSTGRES_USER=appUser    # User name for application roles e.g., 'pguser' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define users of type custom with settings
        - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=appPassword    # Password for application roles e.g., 'password123' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define passwords of type custom with settings
        - PGPASSWORD=appPassword    # Password for application roles e.g., 'password123' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define passwords of type custom with settings
        - POSTGRES_HOST=dbServer    # Host for application roles e.g., 'hostname' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define hosts of type custom with settings
        - PGHOST=appUserHost    # Host for application roles e.g., 'hostname' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define hosts of type custom with settings
        - POSTGRES_PORT=5432    # Port for application roles e.g., 'hostname' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define ports of type custom with settings
        - PGPORT=5432    # Port for application roles e.g., 'hostname' as our database needs, change based on requirements or use any other valid value if defined in Dockerfile   (like “postgres”). You can also define ports of type custom with settings
        - LANG and LC_ALL are used to set the C-locale system for PostgreSQL. We will have same as our application server setup, but you may need them based on requirements or different language/codes in production environment like US English locale etc (as per requirement).  For example : 'en'
        - TZ=Europe/London    # Timezone settings to set the time-zone. You can define your preferred setting here according to server setup and needs of user roles, but make sure that this value matches in both client side app as well if you are using it during docker run or start command (like “postgres”).
